result:
I.  
I keep a small notebook in my left jacket pocket. It’s the size of a modest sandwich—whole wheat, maybe, with a thin slice of cheese. The paper is yellowish, off-brand, the kind that smells faintly of bark when you thumb the edge. I bought it on a rainy afternoon in a town whose name I never learned because the bus broke down and the driver announced we would “wait for the storm to pass,” as though the storm were a dignitary who required patience. I stepped off the coach into a puddle that swallowed my shoe to the ankle, walked three blocks, found a stationer’s shop wedged between a shuttered pharmacy and a bar advertising “coffee & disputes,” and bought the notebook because it was the cheapest thing in the store and I felt obligated to leave with something. The clerk, a woman with silver hair pinned in a crown of victory rolls, rang it up without asking why a drenched stranger needed blank paper at three-thirty on a Tuesday that felt like November but must have been July. She tucked a stub of pencil into the spiral binding. “For your declarations,” she said, as if she knew I would spend the next thousand-odd nights trying to turn the smell of wet asphalt into language.

II.  
Freewriting, they call it. The term is deceptive; it implies a lack of cost, like a giveaway or a garden left untended. But every free thing demands tribute somewhere else. You pay with the acid in your stomach at 2:14 a.m., with the friendships you forget to maintain, with the creeping suspicion that the universe is not unfinished but merely indifferent. Still, I promised myself I would fill every page of the sandwich-sized notebook before I died, and because I’m superstitious I also promised not to calculate how many pages remained. Whenever I open it, I pretend I’m ripping a bandage from the skin of time.  

III.  
Last week I watched a man propose marriage to a traffic light. He knelt on the salted sidewalk, produced a ring that looked suspiciously like a paperclip bent into a circle, and gazed up at the red bulb as though its glow were a private sunrise. Pedestrians swerved around him; phones rose like periscopes. Someone muttered “TikTok,” but the man’s eyes were older than uranium, and when the light changed to green he wept in a way that made me feel I had interrupted the universe’s marriage proposal to itself. I wrote that down, of course. I wrote how the green light looked embarrassed, how the ring glinted like the cheapest star, how I wanted to kneel beside him and confess that I, too, had fallen in love with inanimate instructors—vending machines that refused my crumpled dollar, lampposts that flickered Morse code against my insomnia.  

IV.  
The notebook does not judge. It accepts my handwriting, which devolves from civilization to seismograph as the hour deepens. It accepts the graphite smudge of my left palm, a continent printed nightly across the heel of my hand. Some entries are grocery lists in reverse: I begin with “remember that the body is mostly water” and end with “eggs.” Others are field notes from dreams whose laws of physics I cannot violate even after waking. In one dream I am employed as the wind. My job description: move fragments of a broken lighthouse across an isthmus before the tide returns. I receive minimum wage, paid in the currency of extinct postage stamps. My supervisor is a heron who quotes Rilke in the original German. I wake up clutching the stubby pencil, its eraser worn down to the metal ferrule, and write so fast the letters collide like atoms inside an unlicensed reactor.  

V.  
I have tried, at intervals, to classify the species of silence that occupy an apartment at 4 a.m. There is the silence of the refrigerator, which is not silence at all but a guttural prayer to the god of Freon. There is the silence of books that rearrange themselves alphabetically when no one is looking. There is the silence that follows a heartbeat you weren’t certain belonged to you. I have named them like butterflies: *Coagula insonus*, *Libris mutabilis*, *Cardia incognita*. Naming is a form of ownership, and ownership a form of mercy.  

VI.  
Once I carried the notebook to a hospital waiting room the color of unripe pears. My sister was inside a corridor being told whether her child would inhale without assistance. I sat between a woman knitting a scarf of many colors and a man pricing coffins on his phone. A sign warned “No Food or Drink,” but someone had smuggled in the smell of fried onions. I wrote: “Hope is the onion of the soul; it enlarges while it stings.” A security guard asked to inspect my bag. I showed him the notebook. He riffled the pages, certain I was hiding something sharper than adjectives. Satisfied, he handed it back. Hours later, when the surgeon arrived with news that tasted of copper and dawn, I realized I had written an entire page in the conditional tense: *She would breathe, she would suck air like a secret, she would forgive gravity.*  

VII.  
The problem with writing freely is that freedom becomes addictive. You begin to distrust any sentence that arrives too polished. You pick at it like a scab. You prefer the bleed. Eventually you wonder whether a thought exists if you have not bled it onto paper. Philosophers have argued this for centuries while seated on better chairs. I argue it while stirring ramen with one hand and pinning the notebook open with my elbow, steam ghosting the page.  

VIII.  
I remember the first lie I told the notebook. I wrote: “I am not afraid.” I believed the lie long enough to close the cover, but the graphite kept moving beneath, an underground river eroding the certainty above. Three days later a neighbor knocked to say my car’s headlights were on, though I do not own a car. I stepped outside, saw the headlights extinguished, understood the message was metaphorical, went back upstairs and crossed out the lie. I replaced it with: “I am afraid of metaphors that send me into the night for cars I never bought.” That, at least, was circumstantially honest.  

IX.  
There are days when the world feels like a sweater knitted by someone rage-quitting halfway. Sleeves end without hands, collars gape like shocked mouths. On those days I walk the city wearing the notebook around my neck on a lanyard I braided from red dental floss. I offer strangers the stubby pencil; most decline, fearing contagion of introspection. A few accept. One woman wrote: “I miss the smell of my father’s wallet.” A boy in dinosaur sneakers wrote “egg” and drew an arrow pointing nowhere. A man in an expensive suit wrote a single equation whose variables included “regret” and “interest rate.” I gave them each a page, tore it out carefully along the serrated spiral. They walked away lighter, or so I pretended, because stories need closure the way candles need oxygen.  

X.  
I have never reread the notebook from start to whatever present I occupy. To do so would be to confront a fossil record of my renunciations. Besides, the notebook is not linear; it loops, it spirals, it doubles back like a maze designed by someone who has never met a minotaur. Page forty-seven bleeds into page twelve because both were written on nights when the moon looked subpoenaed. Some pages contain only punctuation: a colon followed by a closed parenthesis, the universal smiley that isn’t smiling. Once I glued a dead moth between two pages, forgot, and weeks later opened to find it had vanished, leaving a powdery constellation. The moth continues somewhere, metamorphosed into illegibility.  

XI.  
Language is a currency that inflates faster than any government can print. The word “awesome” once contained awe; now it contains bubblegum. The word “literally” literally means “figuratively,” and nobody loses sleep except insomniacs with thesaurus addictions. I try to spend my words like an ancient villager bartering eggs for shoes, suspicious of decimal points. The notebook tolerates my miserly nostalgia; it allows me to hoard obsolete adjectives the way elderly relatives stockpile string.  

XII.  
I have written love letters to people I will never meet again: the pharmacist who pronounced my surname correctly, the taxi driver who confessed he’d never driven to the ocean, the woman in laundromat #7 dancing alone to a power ballad about perseverance. I wrote them without envelopes, without expectation, the way plants release oxygen. Perhaps one of them has stumbled across a sentence that survived the trash compactor of days. Perhaps one paused, mid-breath, outside a bodega, and felt the universe tip its hat.  

XIII.  
Time’s most underhanded trick is its refusal to announce conversions. Twenty minutes of waiting for a delayed train equals one lifetime of a mayfly, but nobody posts flyers. I try to keep tabs by marking margins: the date, the phase of the moon, the temperature of my doubt. The measurements contradict each other like drunks arguing last call. I draw Venn diagrams that never intersect; I draw clocks whose hands point to verbs. Still, the train never arrives on the page.  

XIV.  
Some nights I attempt to transcribe the sound of snow falling on snow. The challenge is not the listening but the betrayal of transcription. Snow on snow is the hush of a librarian shelving the last book before apocalypse, a hush that contains multitudinous microscopic collisions, each flake surrendering its individuality like monks taking vows. The notebook receives my best onomatopoeia and gives back nothing but graphite, mute and dark.  

XV.  
I have never confessed the crime I committed at sixteen: I broke into the school after hours and rearranged the library’s card catalog so that “loneliness” filed under “L” now sat between “lighthouses” and “lunchboxes.” I wanted to see if students browsing boredom would stumble upon beacons. I wanted to weaponize serendipity. Did it work? I graduated before longitudinal data. Still, I imagine some sophomore thumbing through, finding the card, feeling the vertiginous spark that someone else has felt exactly this, here, now.  

XVI.  
The notebook is almost full. I measure this not by remaining pages but by the thickness of the pencil, now a nub I grip with thumb and forefinger like a surgical instrument. I could buy another pencil—they sell them in twelve-packs for the cost of a prayer—but superstition forbids replacement. If I run out before the notebook, perhaps the world declares me complete. If the notebook outlives the pencil, perhaps I am allowed to persist.  

XVII.  
I keep thinking there will be a final sentence, a coda brilliant as a coin revealed from behind the ear. Instead, there is only the next sentence, which arrives wet, blind, amphibian. I write: “The future is a flashlight taped to the forehead of a terrified volunteer.” I write: “Forgive the ocean; it is only practicing.” I write the word “yes” until the y curves into a question mark that refuses to dot its own eye.  

XVIII.  
Tonight the city outside my window conducts a symphony of jackhammers and sirens. The notebook listens. I offer it my pulse, my grocery list, my recurring dream of ladders that lead sideways. I offer it the traffic-light suitor, the moth dust, the equation of regret. I offer it this last breath, which begins as always: I keep a small notebook in my left jacket pocket…

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
